hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Semi-detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, brick cladding, first storey bay windows, decorative wooden gable pediments, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its semi-detached bay-and-gable house-form typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, bay window.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Constructed as a pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, brick cladding, first storey bay windows, decorative wooden gable pediments, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: Two-storey semi-detached bay-and-gable house, c. 1890-1903, paired with 44 Nassau St. Running-bond brick with decorative brickwork and a double-height canted projecting bay. Cross-gable roof with decorative wooden bargeboard. Party walls on both sides; 3 dwelling units. Fair condition with missing elements and damaged masonry.
heritage_summary: Constructed as a pair, the properties retain most of their original building features including cross-gabled rooflines, brick cladding, first storey bay windows, decorative wooden gable pediments, and fenestration pattern with segmentally arched openings. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.
